Description: Iraqi forces invade Kuwait, triggering the Gulf War and removing a major OPEC producer's exports from the market.
Sustained period of low prices through the mid-1990s driven by ample non-OPEC supply and weak Asian demand growth.
Currency and banking crisis across East Asia sharply reduces regional oil demand, contributing to the 1998 price collapse to under $10/barrel.
OPEC and non-OPEC producers (incl. Mexico, Norway) agree to significant output cuts, helping prices recover from the 1998 lows.
Attacks on the US trigger a global demand shock and risk-off sentiment, with a short-lived but sharp price reaction.
A national strike paralyzes Venezuela's state oil company, removing significant crude exports from the market into early 2003.
The Iraq War begins, raising fears of prolonged supply disruption from a major OPEC producer.
Oil prices peak amid strong emerging-market demand, a weak US dollar, and speculative activity, just before the global financial crisis.

Event: Lehman Brothers collapse / Global Financial Crisis
Description: The GFC triggers a collapse in global demand expectations; Brent falls from ~$147 to under $40 within six months.
Uprisings spread across North Africa and the Middle East (Tunisia, Egypt, Libya, Syria), raising supply-disruption risk across producer states.
Civil conflict shuts in the majority of Libya's oil production and exports for most of 2011.

Event: EU and US sanctions tighten on Iran
Description: Escalating sanctions over Iran's nuclear program curb Iranian crude exports, tightening global supply.
ISIS captures Mosul and advances toward northern Iraqi oil infrastructure, raising near-term supply-risk premium.
OPEC (led by Saudi Arabia) maintains output despite oversupply from US shale growth, initiating the 2014-2016 price collapse (Brent falls from ~$115 to ~$30).
Implementation Day of the Iran nuclear deal lifts sanctions, allowing Iranian crude back onto the market and pressuring prices.
OPEC agrees with non-OPEC producers (notably Russia) to cut output for the first time since 2008, supporting a price recovery.

Event: US withdraws from Iran nuclear deal
Description: The US reinstates sanctions on Iranian oil exports, tightening expected supply.

Event: Drone/missile attack on Saudi Aramco facilities
Description: Attacks on the Abqaiq and Khurais facilities knock out roughly half of Saudi Arabia's oil output temporarily, causing the largest single-day price spike in Brent's history.
Russia refuses proposed OPEC+ cuts; Saudi Arabia responds by ramping production and slashing prices, crashing the market amid collapsing COVID-19 demand.
OPEC+ agrees to a historic ~9.7 million bpd output cut to stabilize prices after the COVID-19 demand collapse (WTI briefly turns negative in April 2020).
Russia's invasion triggers sanctions on Russian energy exports and fears of a major supply disruption, pushing Brent above $100/barrel.
